Made with MyST

The Multivariate Normal Distribution

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Bayesian Modeling and Bayesian Analysis of the Normal Distribution. Familiarity with matrix algebra and multivariate calculus is assumed.

The multivariate normal (MVN) distribution — also called the multivariate Gaussian — is the workhorse of probabilistic machine learning. It is one of the few distributions in more than one dimension that we can work with analytically, and it arises naturally as the limiting distribution of sums of independent random vectors (the multivariate central limit theorem). Many of the models we study in this course are built from MVN distributions as building blocks.

In this lecture we cover:

  • The MVN distribution: generative story, density, marginals, conditionals, linear Gaussian models

  • Maximum likelihood estimation

  • The Wishart and inverse Wishart distributions

  • Bayesian estimation with a normal-inverse-Wishart (NIW) prior

  • Posterior marginals: the multivariate Student’s t distribution

Source
import torch
from torch.distributions import Normal, MultivariateNormal, Wishart
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


def plot_cov(Σ, μ=None, ax=None, **kwargs):
    """Visualize a 2D covariance matrix as a 1-σ ellipse."""
    D = Σ.shape[-1]
    μ = torch.zeros(D) if μ is None else μ
    λ_diag, U = torch.linalg.eigh(Σ)
    zs = torch.column_stack([
        torch.cos(torch.linspace(0, 2 * torch.pi, 50)),
        torch.sin(torch.linspace(0, 2 * torch.pi, 50))
    ])
    xs = (zs * torch.sqrt(λ_diag)) @ U.T
    ax = plt.axes(aspect=1) if ax is None else ax
    ax.plot(μ[0] + xs[:, 0], μ[1] + xs[:, 1], **kwargs)

Generative Story¶

The cleanest way to understand the MVN is to build it up from scratch. Start with a vector of standard normal random variates, z=[z1,…,zD]⊤\mbz = [z_1, \ldots, z_D]^\topz=[z1​,…,zD​]⊤, where each component is drawn independently,

zd∼iidN(0,1)for d=1,…,D.\begin{align} z_d &\iid{\sim} \cN(0, 1) \quad \text{for } d = 1, \ldots, D. \end{align}zd​​∼iidN(0,1)for d=1,…,D.​​
(1)

This DDD-dimensional random variable is the simplest possible multivariate Gaussian, but it is not very interesting — all the coordinates are independent and have unit variance. Its joint density factors as a product of univariate Gaussians,

p(z)=∏d=1DN(zd∣0,1)=∏d=1D12πe−12zd2=(2π)−D2exp⁡{−12∑d=1Dzd2}=(2π)−D2exp⁡{−12z⊤z}≜N(z∣0,I).\begin{align} p(\mbz) &= \prod_{d=1}^D \cN(z_d \mid 0, 1) \\ &= \prod_{d=1}^D \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2} z_d^2} \\ &= (2 \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} \exp\left\{ -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{d=1}^D z_d^2 \right\} \\ &= (2 \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} \exp\left\{ -\frac{1}{2} \mbz^\top \mbz \right\} \\ &\triangleq \cN(\mbz \mid \mbzero, \mbI). \end{align}p(z)​=d=1∏D​N(zd​∣0,1)=d=1∏D​2π​1​e−21​zd2​=(2π)−2D​exp{−21​d=1∑D​zd2​}=(2π)−2D​exp{−21​z⊤z}≜N(z∣0,I).​​
(2)

The last step recognizes this as a multivariate normal with zero mean and identity covariance. In D=2D=2D=2 dimensions, the contours of constant density are circles centered at the origin — hence the name spherical Gaussian.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

z1, z2 = torch.meshgrid(
    torch.linspace(-4, 4, 50),
    torch.linspace(-4, 4, 50),
    indexing='ij',
)
log_p = Normal(0, 1).log_prob(z1) + Normal(0, 1).log_prob(z2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(z1, z2, log_p)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$z_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$z_2$")
ax.set_aspect(1)
plt.tight_layout()

We can obtain more interesting joint distributions by linearly transforming this random vector. Let U\mbUU be an orthogonal D×DD \times DD×D matrix (so U⊤U=I\mbU^\top \mbU = \mbIU⊤U=I) whose columns are the eigenvectors of a desired covariance, and let Λ=diag([λ1,…,λD])\mbLambda = \diag([\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_D])Λ=diag([λ1​,…,λD​]) with λd>0\lambda_d > 0λd​>0 be a diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. Define,

x=UΛ12z.\begin{align} \mbx = \mbU \mbLambda^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz. \end{align}x=UΛ21​z.​​
(3)

The matrix U\mbUU rotates the coordinate axes, while Λ1/2\mbLambda^{1/2}Λ1/2 stretches each axis by a factor of λd\sqrt{\lambda_d}λd​​. The result is a random vector whose variance along the ddd-th eigenvector is λd\lambda_dλd​.

Exercise

Compute the mean E[x]\E[\mbx]E[x] and covariance Cov[x]=E[(x−E[x])(x−E[x])⊤]\mathrm{Cov}[\mbx] = \E[(\mbx - \E[\mbx])(\mbx - \E[\mbx])^\top]Cov[x]=E[(x−E[x])(x−E[x])⊤].

Density of the MVN¶

To find the density of x=UΛ1/2z\mbx = \mbU \mbLambda^{1/2} \mbzx=UΛ1/2z, we apply the multivariate change-of-variables formula. Since x\mbxx is an invertible linear function of z\mbzz, with z=Λ−1/2U⊤x\mbz = \mbLambda^{-1/2} \mbU^\top \mbxz=Λ−1/2U⊤x, the density transforms as,

p(x)=p(z)∣ ⁣dz ⁣dx∣=p(Λ−12U⊤x) ∣Λ−12U⊤∣=(2π)−D2exp⁡{−12x⊤UΛ−1U⊤x}∣Λ−12∣ ∣U⊤∣=(2π)−D2exp⁡{−12x⊤UΛ−1U⊤x}∣Λ∣−12=(2π)−D2exp⁡{−12x⊤Σ−1x}∣Σ∣−12\begin{align} p(\mbx) &= p(\mbz) \left| \frac{\dif \mbz}{\dif \mbx} \right| \\ &= p(\mbLambda^{-\frac{1}{2}} \mbU^\top \mbx) \, |\mbLambda^{-\frac{1}{2}} \mbU^\top| \\ &= (2 \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} \exp \left\{ -\frac{1}{2} \mbx^\top \mbU \mbLambda^{-1} \mbU^\top \mbx \right \} |\mbLambda^{-\frac{1}{2}}| \, |\mbU^\top| \\ &= (2 \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} \exp \left\{ -\frac{1}{2} \mbx^\top \mbU \mbLambda^{-1} \mbU^\top \mbx \right \} |\mbLambda|^{-\frac{1}{2}} \\ &= (2 \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} \exp \left\{ -\frac{1}{2} \mbx^\top \mbSigma^{-1} \mbx \right \} |\mbSigma|^{-\frac{1}{2}} \end{align}p(x)​=p(z)∣∣​dxdz​∣∣​=p(Λ−21​U⊤x)∣Λ−21​U⊤∣=(2π)−2D​exp{−21​x⊤UΛ−1U⊤x}∣Λ−21​∣∣U⊤∣=(2π)−2D​exp{−21​x⊤UΛ−1U⊤x}∣Λ∣−21​=(2π)−2D​exp{−21​x⊤Σ−1x}∣Σ∣−21​​​
(4)

where Σ=UΛU⊤\mbSigma = \mbU \mbLambda \mbU^\topΣ=UΛU⊤. The key steps are: (i) the Jacobian of the linear map is the absolute value of the determinant of Λ−1/2U⊤\mbLambda^{-1/2} \mbU^\topΛ−1/2U⊤; (ii) since U\mbUU is orthogonal, ∣U⊤∣=1|\mbU^\top| = 1∣U⊤∣=1; and (iii) ∣Λ−1/2∣=∣Λ∣−1/2|\mbLambda^{-1/2}| = |\mbLambda|^{-1/2}∣Λ−1/2∣=∣Λ∣−1/2 since Λ\mbLambdaΛ is diagonal.

Adding a translation so that x=UΛ12z+μ\mbx = \mbU \mbLambda^{\frac{1}{2}}\mbz + \mbmux=UΛ21​z+μ for μ∈RD\mbmu \in \reals^Dμ∈RD, the same argument gives the general MVN density,

p(x)=(2π)−D2∣Σ∣−12exp⁡{−12(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ)}≜N(x∣μ,Σ).\begin{align} p(\mbx) &= (2\pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} |\mbSigma|^{-\frac{1}{2}} \exp\left\{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbx - \mbmu)^\top \mbSigma^{-1} (\mbx - \mbmu) \right\} \triangleq \cN(\mbx \mid \mbmu, \mbSigma). \end{align}p(x)​=(2π)−2D​∣Σ∣−21​exp{−21​(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ)}≜N(x∣μ,Σ).​​
(5)

This is the pdf of the multivariate normal (MVN) distribution with mean vector μ∈RD\mbmu \in \reals^Dμ∈RD and positive definite covariance matrix Σ∈RD×D\mbSigma \in \reals^{D \times D}Σ∈RD×D.

The density depends on x\mbxx only through the squared Mahalanobis distance,

Δ2=(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ).\begin{align} \Delta^2 &= (\mbx - \mbmu)^\top \mbSigma^{-1} (\mbx - \mbmu). \end{align}Δ2​=(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ).​​
(6)

This is a generalization of the squared Euclidean distance that accounts for the scale and orientation of the distribution. Points x\mbxx at equal Mahalanobis distance from μ\mbmuμ lie on an ellipse whose axes are aligned with the eigenvectors of Σ\mbSigmaΣ and whose radii are proportional to λd\sqrt{\lambda_d}λd​​.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

# Eigenvectors (rotation by π/4)
θ  = torch.tensor(torch.pi / 4)
u1 = torch.stack([torch.cos(θ),  torch.sin(θ)])
u2 = torch.stack([-torch.sin(θ), torch.cos(θ)])
U  = torch.column_stack([u1, u2])

# Eigenvalues
λ1 = torch.tensor(2.0**2)
λ2 = torch.tensor(0.5**2)
Λ  = torch.tensor([λ1, λ2])

# One iso-contour in z-space, mapped to x-space
zs = torch.column_stack([
    torch.cos(torch.linspace(0, 2 * torch.pi, 50)),
    torch.sin(torch.linspace(0, 2 * torch.pi, 50))
])
xs = (zs * torch.sqrt(Λ)) @ U.T

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs[:, 0], xs[:, 1], ':')
ax.annotate("", xy=(torch.sqrt(λ1) * u1).tolist(), xytext=(0, 0),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='k', lw=2))
ax.text(torch.sqrt(λ1).item() * u1[0].item(),
        torch.sqrt(λ1).item() * u1[1].item() + .4,
        r"$\sqrt{\lambda_1}\,\mathbf{u}_1$")
ax.annotate("", xy=(torch.sqrt(λ2) * u2).tolist(), xytext=(0, 0),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='k', lw=2))
ax.text(torch.sqrt(λ2).item() * u2[0].item() - 1.5,
        torch.sqrt(λ2).item() * u2[1].item(),
        r"$\sqrt{\lambda_2}\,\mathbf{u}_2$")
ax.set_xlim(-3, 3)
ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
ax.set_aspect(1)
plt.tight_layout()

Generative Story (General Form)¶

The construction above used a specific structure for the transformation matrix — an orthogonal matrix times a diagonal scaling. But we could apply any linear transformation to the standard normal,

x=Az+μ\begin{align} \mbx &= \mbA \mbz + \mbmu \end{align}x​=Az+μ​​
(7)

for A∈RM×D\mbA \in \reals^{M \times D}A∈RM×D and μ∈RM\mbmu \in \reals^Mμ∈RM. Since x\mbxx is an affine function of a Gaussian, it too is Gaussian, x∼N(μ,Σ)\mbx \sim \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma)x∼N(μ,Σ), with covariance

Σ=E[(x−μ)(x−μ)⊤]=AE[zz⊤]A⊤=AA⊤.\begin{align} \mbSigma = \E[(\mbx - \mbmu)(\mbx - \mbmu)^\top] = \mbA \E[\mbz \mbz^\top] \mbA^\top = \mbA \mbA^\top. \end{align}Σ=E[(x−μ)(x−μ)⊤]=AE[zz⊤]A⊤=AA⊤.​​
(8)

This is sometimes called the square root form of the MVN — A\mbAA is a square root of the covariance matrix. When M>DM > DM>D, the covariance Σ=AA⊤\mbSigma = \mbA \mbA^\topΣ=AA⊤ has rank at most D<MD < MD<M, so it is only positive semidefinite. This yields a degenerate multivariate normal whose density does not exist with respect to Lebesgue measure on RM\reals^MRM; instead, the distribution is supported on a DDD-dimensional subspace.

To sample x∼N(μ,Σ)\mbx \sim \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma)x∼N(μ,Σ) in practice, we need to find a square root Σ12∈RD×D\mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}} \in \reals^{D \times D}Σ21​∈RD×D such that Σ=(Σ12)(Σ12)⊤\mbSigma = (\mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}})(\mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}})^\topΣ=(Σ21​)(Σ21​)⊤, then draw z∼N(0,I)\mbz \sim \cN(\mbzero, \mbI)z∼N(0,I) and set x=Σ12z+μ\mbx = \mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz + \mbmux=Σ21​z+μ. Common choices include:

  • Eigendecomposition: Σ12=UΛ12\mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}} = \mbU \mbLambda^{\frac{1}{2}}Σ21​=UΛ21​ where Σ=UΛU⊤\mbSigma = \mbU \mbLambda \mbU^\topΣ=UΛU⊤. This is geometrically natural but more expensive to compute.

  • Cholesky decomposition: Σ12=chol(Σ)\mbSigma^{\frac{1}{2}} = \mathrm{chol}(\mbSigma)Σ21​=chol(Σ), the unique lower-triangular square root. This is numerically efficient and is the standard choice in practice.

Linear Transformations¶

A key property that makes the MVN so tractable is that it is closed under affine transformations. If x∼N(μ,Σ)\mbx \sim \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma)x∼N(μ,Σ) and A∈RM×D\mbA \in \reals^{M \times D}A∈RM×D, b∈RM\mbb \in \reals^Mb∈RM, then,

Ax+b∼N(Aμ+b,AΣA⊤).\begin{align} \mbA \mbx + \mbb \sim \cN(\mbA \mbmu + \mbb, \mbA \mbSigma \mbA^\top). \end{align}Ax+b∼N(Aμ+b,AΣA⊤).​​
(9)

This follows immediately from the generative story: Ax+b=A(Σ1/2z+μ)+b=(AΣ1/2)z+(Aμ+b)\mbA \mbx + \mbb = \mbA (\mbSigma^{1/2} \mbz + \mbmu) + \mbb = (\mbA \mbSigma^{1/2}) \mbz + (\mbA \mbmu + \mbb)Ax+b=A(Σ1/2z+μ)+b=(AΣ1/2)z+(Aμ+b), which is an affine function of the standard normal z\mbzz. The mean transforms linearly and the covariance transforms by a congruence. This property means, for instance, that projecting a multivariate normal onto any subspace gives a (lower-dimensional) multivariate normal.

Marginal Distributions¶

Suppose we have a joint MVN on a vector x∈RD\mbx \in \reals^Dx∈RD and we want to integrate out some of the variables. Partition x\mbxx and the MVN parameters into two subsets aaa and bbb,

x=[xaxb],μ=[μaμb],Σ=[ΣaaΣabΣbaΣbb].\begin{align} \mbx = \begin{bmatrix} \mbx_a \\ \mbx_b \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mbmu = \begin{bmatrix} \mbmu_a \\ \mbmu_b \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mbSigma = \begin{bmatrix} \mbSigma_{aa} & \mbSigma_{ab} \\ \mbSigma_{ba} & \mbSigma_{bb} \end{bmatrix}. \end{align}x=[xa​xb​​],μ=[μa​μb​​],Σ=[Σaa​Σba​​Σab​Σbb​​].​​
(10)

The block Σaa\mbSigma_{aa}Σaa​ is the covariance of xa\mbx_axa​ with itself, Σbb\mbSigma_{bb}Σbb​ is the covariance of xb\mbx_bxb​ with itself, and Σab=Σba⊤\mbSigma_{ab} = \mbSigma_{ba}^\topΣab​=Σba⊤​ captures the cross-covariances between the two subsets.

Marginalizing over xb\mbx_bxb​ is equivalent to applying the linear transformation A=[I0]\mbA = \begin{bmatrix}\mbI & \mbzero \end{bmatrix}A=[I​0​] (an identity block followed by zeros) so that Ax=xa\mbA \mbx = \mbx_aAx=xa​. By the closure under linear transformations,

p(xa)=N(Aμ,AΣA⊤)=N(μa,Σaa).\begin{align} p(\mbx_a) &= \cN\left(\mbA \mbmu, \mbA \mbSigma \mbA^\top \right) = \cN(\mbmu_a, \mbSigma_{aa}). \end{align}p(xa​)​=N(Aμ,AΣA⊤)=N(μa​,Σaa​).​​
(11)

This is a remarkably simple result: to obtain the marginal of a subset of variables, just extract the corresponding blocks of the mean vector and covariance matrix. There is no integration needed — the marginal is determined directly by the parameters of the joint distribution.

Conditional Distributions¶

Conditioning is more involved than marginalization, but the MVN remains tractable. We want to find p(xa∣xb)p(\mbx_a \mid \mbx_b)p(xa​∣xb​). It helps to work with the precision matrix (the inverse of the covariance), Σ−1≜Λ\mbSigma^{-1} \triangleq \mbLambdaΣ−1≜Λ, written in the same block form,

Λ=[ΛaaΛabΛbaΛbb].\begin{align} \mbLambda = \begin{bmatrix} \mbLambda_{aa} & \mbLambda_{ab} \\ \mbLambda_{ba} & \mbLambda_{bb} \end{bmatrix}. \end{align}Λ=[Λaa​Λba​​Λab​Λbb​​].​​
(12)

The blocks of Λ\mbLambdaΛ are related to the blocks of Σ\mbSigmaΣ via the Schur complement. Specifically,

Λaa=(Σaa−ΣabΣbb−1Σba)−1,Λab=−ΛaaΣabΣbb−1.\begin{align} \mbLambda_{aa} &= (\mbSigma_{aa} - \mbSigma_{ab} \mbSigma_{bb}^{-1} \mbSigma_{ba})^{-1}, \\ \mbLambda_{ab} &= -\mbLambda_{aa} \mbSigma_{ab} \mbSigma_{bb}^{-1}. \end{align}Λaa​Λab​​=(Σaa​−Σab​Σbb−1​Σba​)−1,=−Λaa​Σab​Σbb−1​.​​
(13)

The quantity Σaa−ΣabΣbb−1Σba\mbSigma_{aa} - \mbSigma_{ab} \mbSigma_{bb}^{-1} \mbSigma_{ba}Σaa​−Σab​Σbb−1​Σba​ is the Schur complement of Σbb\mbSigma_{bb}Σbb​ in Σ\mbSigmaΣ. It will appear again as the conditional covariance.

To find the conditional distribution, we treat xb\mbx_bxb​ as fixed and expand the joint log density as a function of xa\mbx_axa​ alone. Using Bayes’ rule and the block precision matrix,

p(xa∣xb)∝p(xa,xb)=N(x∣μ,Σ)∝exp⁡{−12(x−μ)⊤Λ(x−μ)}∝exp⁡{−12(xa−μa)⊤Λaa(xa−μa)−(xa−μa)⊤Λab(xb−μb)}∝exp⁡{−12xa⊤Ja∣bxa+xa⊤ha∣b},\begin{align} p(\mbx_a \mid \mbx_b) &\propto p(\mbx_a, \mbx_b) = \cN(\mbx \mid \mbmu, \mbSigma) \\ &\propto \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbx - \mbmu)^\top \mbLambda (\mbx - \mbmu) \right\} \\ &\propto \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbx_a - \mbmu_a)^\top \mbLambda_{aa} (\mbx_a - \mbmu_a) - (\mbx_a - \mbmu_a)^\top \mbLambda_{ab} (\mbx_b - \mbmu_b) \right\} \\ &\propto \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \mbx_a^\top \mbJ_{a|b} \mbx_{a} + \mbx_a^\top \mbh_{a|b} \right\}, \end{align}p(xa​∣xb​)​∝p(xa​,xb​)=N(x∣μ,Σ)∝exp{−21​(x−μ)⊤Λ(x−μ)}∝exp{−21​(xa​−μa​)⊤Λaa​(xa​−μa​)−(xa​−μa​)⊤Λab​(xb​−μb​)}∝exp{−21​xa⊤​Ja∣b​xa​+xa⊤​ha∣b​},​​
(14)

where we collected terms quadratic and linear in xa\mbx_axa​, defining the information matrix Ja∣b=Λaa\mbJ_{a|b} = \mbLambda_{aa}Ja∣b​=Λaa​ and information vector ha∣b=Λaaμa−Λab(xb−μb)\mbh_{a|b} = \mbLambda_{aa} \mbmu_a - \mbLambda_{ab} (\mbx_b - \mbmu_b)ha∣b​=Λaa​μa​−Λab​(xb​−μb​). The exponent is a quadratic in xa\mbx_axa​, which means the conditional is also Gaussian — this is the information form of the conditional density.

Completing the square (as in Lecture 1), we convert from information form back to the standard mean-covariance form and find,

p(xa∣xb)=N(xa∣μa∣b,Σa∣b),\begin{align} p(\mbx_a \mid \mbx_b) &= \cN(\mbx_a \mid \mbmu_{a | b}, \mbSigma_{a|b}), \end{align}p(xa​∣xb​)​=N(xa​∣μa∣b​,Σa∣b​),​​
(15)

where the conditional covariance and mean are,

Σa∣b=Ja∣b−1=Λaa−1=Σaa−ΣabΣbb−1Σba,\begin{align} \mbSigma_{a|b} &= \mbJ_{a|b}^{-1} = \mbLambda_{aa}^{-1} = \mbSigma_{aa} - \mbSigma_{ab} \mbSigma_{bb}^{-1} \mbSigma_{ba}, \end{align}Σa∣b​​=Ja∣b−1​=Λaa−1​=Σaa​−Σab​Σbb−1​Σba​,​​
(16)
μa∣b=Ja∣b−1ha∣b=μa+ΣabΣbb−1(xb−μb).\begin{align} \mbmu_{a|b} &= \mbJ_{a|b}^{-1} \mbh_{a|b} = \mbmu_a + \mbSigma_{ab} \mbSigma_{bb}^{-1} (\mbx_b - \mbmu_b). \end{align}μa∣b​​=Ja∣b−1​ha∣b​=μa​+Σab​Σbb−1​(xb​−μb​).​​
(17)

Two properties of these formulas are worth noting. First, the conditional covariance Σa∣b\mbSigma_{a|b}Σa∣b​ does not depend on the observed value xb\mbx_bxb​ — conditioning on xb\mbx_bxb​ reduces our uncertainty about xa\mbx_axa​ by a fixed amount regardless of what xb\mbx_bxb​ turns out to be. Second, the conditional mean μa∣b\mbmu_{a|b}μa∣b​ is a linear function of xb\mbx_bxb​: it starts at the marginal mean μa\mbmu_aμa​ and shifts by an amount proportional to how much xb\mbx_bxb​ deviates from its own marginal mean μb\mbmu_bμb​. The matrix ΣabΣbb−1\mbSigma_{ab}\mbSigma_{bb}^{-1}Σab​Σbb−1​ is the multivariate analog of the regression coefficient in simple linear regression.

Linear Gaussian Models¶

A linear Gaussian model describes a situation where one variable is a noisy linear function of another. Specifically, suppose

x∼N(b,Q),y∣x∼N(Cx+d,R).\begin{align} \mbx &\sim \cN(\mbb, \mbQ), \\ \mby \mid \mbx &\sim \cN(\mbC \mbx + \mbd, \mbR). \end{align}xy∣x​∼N(b,Q),∼N(Cx+d,R).​​
(18)

Here x∈RDx\mbx \in \reals^{D_x}x∈RDx​ is a latent (unobserved) variable with prior mean b\mbbb and covariance Q\mbQQ, and y∈RDy\mby \in \reals^{D_y}y∈RDy​ is an observation generated by applying a linear map C\mbCC to x\mbxx, adding a bias d\mbdd, and corrupting with Gaussian noise of covariance R\mbRR. This model appears throughout this course: as a factor analysis model (when Dx<DyD_x < D_yDx​<Dy​), as the observation model in a Kalman filter, and as a building block for Gaussian process regression.

The key question is: what is the joint distribution p(x,y)p(\mbx, \mby)p(x,y)? We can answer this by writing x\mbxx and y\mbyy as explicit linear functions of independent standard normals. Let zx∼N(0,IDx)\mbz_x \sim \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{D_x})zx​∼N(0,IDx​​) and zy∼N(0,IDy)\mbz_y \sim \cN(\mbzero, \mbI_{D_y})zy​∼N(0,IDy​​) be independent. Then,

x=b+Q12zx,y=Cx+d+R12zy=(Cb+d)+CQ12zx+R12zy.\begin{align} \mbx &= \mbb + \mbQ^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz_x, \\ \mby &= \mbC \mbx + \mbd + \mbR^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz_y = (\mbC \mbb + \mbd) + \mbC \mbQ^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz_x + \mbR^{\frac{1}{2}} \mbz_y. \end{align}xy​=b+Q21​zx​,=Cx+d+R21​zy​=(Cb+d)+CQ21​zx​+R21​zy​.​​
(19)

Stacking into a single vector, we can write (x,y)(\mbx, \mby)(x,y) as an affine function of the independent standard normals (zx,zy)(\mbz_x, \mbz_y)(zx​,zy​),

[xy]=[bCb+d]+[Q120CQ12R12][zxzy].\begin{align} \begin{bmatrix} \mbx \\ \mby \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \mbb \\ \mbC \mbb + \mbd \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \mbQ^{\frac{1}{2}} & \mbzero \\ \mbC \mbQ^{\frac{1}{2}} & \mbR^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mbz_x \\ \mbz_y \end{bmatrix}. \end{align}[xy​]​=[bCb+d​]+[Q21​CQ21​​0R21​​][zx​zy​​].​​
(20)

Since this is an affine function of a standard normal, the joint distribution is Gaussian. Using the formula Σ=AA⊤\mbSigma = \mbA \mbA^\topΣ=AA⊤ for the covariance,

[xy]∼N ⁣([bCb+d],  [QQC⊤CQCQC⊤+R]).\begin{align} \begin{bmatrix} \mbx \\ \mby \end{bmatrix} \sim \cN\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \mbb \\ \mbC \mbb + \mbd \end{bmatrix},\; \begin{bmatrix} \mbQ & \mbQ \mbC^\top \\ \mbC \mbQ & \mbC \mbQ \mbC^\top + \mbR \end{bmatrix} \right). \end{align}[xy​]∼N([bCb+d​],[QCQ​QC⊤CQC⊤+R​]).​​
(21)

Reading off the blocks, we see that the marginal distribution of y\mbyy is N(Cb+d, CQC⊤+R)\cN(\mbC \mbb + \mbd,\, \mbC \mbQ \mbC^\top + \mbR)N(Cb+d,CQC⊤+R). The cross-covariance QC⊤\mbQ \mbC^\topQC⊤ reflects the fact that x\mbxx and y\mbyy share the common noise term Q1/2zx\mbQ^{1/2}\mbz_xQ1/2zx​. Given the joint distribution, we can also compute the conditional distribution p(x∣y)p(\mbx \mid \mby)p(x∣y) using the formulas from the previous section — this is the core of Bayesian linear regression and the Kalman filter.

Maximum Likelihood Estimation¶

Given NNN i.i.d. observations x1,…,xN∼iidN(μ,Σ)\mbx_1, \ldots, \mbx_N \iid{\sim} \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma)x1​,…,xN​∼iidN(μ,Σ), we can estimate the parameters (μ,Σ)(\mbmu, \mbSigma)(μ,Σ) by maximum likelihood. The log likelihood is,

L(μ,Σ)=∑n=1Nlog⁡N(xn∣μ,Σ)=−ND2log⁡(2π)−N2log⁡∣Σ∣−12∑n=1N(xn−μ)⊤Σ−1(xn−μ).\begin{align} \cL(\mbmu, \mbSigma) &= \sum_{n=1}^N \log \cN(\mbx_n \mid \mbmu, \mbSigma) \\ &= -\frac{ND}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2}\log|\mbSigma| - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbSigma^{-1} (\mbx_n - \mbmu). \end{align}L(μ,Σ)​=n=1∑N​logN(xn​∣μ,Σ)=−2ND​log(2π)−2N​log∣Σ∣−21​n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)⊤Σ−1(xn​−μ).​​
(22)
Exercise

Take gradients of L(μ,Σ)\cL(\mbmu, \mbSigma)L(μ,Σ) with respect to μ\mbmuμ and Σ−1\mbSigma^{-1}Σ−1 and set them to zero to show that the maximum likelihood estimates are,

μML=1N∑n=1Nxn,ΣML=1N∑n=1N(xn−μML)(xn−μML)⊤.\begin{align} \mbmu_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N \mbx_n, \\ \mbSigma_{\mathsf{ML}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n - \mbmu_{\mathsf{ML}}) (\mbx_n - \mbmu_{\mathsf{ML}})^\top. \end{align}μML​ΣML​​=N1​n=1∑N​xn​,=N1​n=1∑N​(xn​−μML​)(xn​−μML​)⊤.​​
(23)

Hint: use the identities ∂∂μ(x−μ)⊤A(x−μ)=−2A(x−μ)\frac{\partial}{\partial \mbmu} (\mbx - \mbmu)^\top \mbA (\mbx - \mbmu) = -2\mbA(\mbx - \mbmu)∂μ∂​(x−μ)⊤A(x−μ)=−2A(x−μ) and ∂∂Alog⁡∣A∣=A−⊤\frac{\partial}{\partial \mbA} \log |\mbA| = \mbA^{-\top}∂A∂​log∣A∣=A−⊤.

The MLE for the mean is the sample mean, and the MLE for the covariance is the sample covariance (with a factor of 1/N1/N1/N rather than 1/(N−1)1/(N-1)1/(N−1), so it is slightly biased downward for finite NNN). Both are intuitive and easy to compute.

Bayesian Estimation: Unknown Mean¶

Suppose the covariance Σ\mbSigmaΣ is known but the mean μ\mbmuμ is unknown. As in Lecture 1, we place a Gaussian prior on μ\mbmuμ and compute the posterior,

μ∼N(μ0,Σ0),xn∼iidN(μ,Σ).\begin{align} \mbmu &\sim \cN(\mbmu_0, \mbSigma_0), \\ \mbx_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma). \end{align}μxn​​∼N(μ0​,Σ0​),∼iidN(μ,Σ).​​
(24)

The hyperparameters η=(μ0,Σ0,Σ)\mbeta = (\mbmu_0, \mbSigma_0, \mbSigma)η=(μ0​,Σ0​,Σ) encode our prior beliefs: μ0\mbmu_0μ0​ is our prior guess for the mean, and Σ0\mbSigma_0Σ0​ captures how uncertain we are about that guess (Σ0−1\mbSigma_0^{-1}Σ0−1​ is the prior precision). Our goal is to compute the posterior p(μ∣X,η)p(\mbmu \mid \mbX, \mbeta)p(μ∣X,η).

By Bayes’ rule, the posterior is proportional to the prior times the likelihood. Expanding the exponents and collecting terms quadratic and linear in μ\mbmuμ,

p(μ∣X,η)∝N(μ∣μ0,Σ0)∏n=1NN(xn∣μ,Σ)∝exp⁡{−12(μ−μ0)⊤Σ0−1(μ−μ0)}∏n=1Nexp⁡{−12(xn−μ)⊤Σ−1(xn−μ)}∝exp⁡{−12μ⊤JNμ+μ⊤hN},\begin{align} p(\mbmu \mid \mbX, \mbeta) &\propto \cN(\mbmu \mid \mbmu_0, \mbSigma_0) \prod_{n=1}^N \cN(\mbx_n \mid \mbmu, \mbSigma) \\ &\propto \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbmu - \mbmu_0)^\top \mbSigma_0^{-1} (\mbmu - \mbmu_0) \right\} \prod_{n=1}^N \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbSigma^{-1} (\mbx_n - \mbmu) \right\} \\ &\propto \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \mbmu^\top \mbJ_N \mbmu + \mbmu^\top \mbh_N \right\}, \end{align}p(μ∣X,η)​∝N(μ∣μ0​,Σ0​)n=1∏N​N(xn​∣μ,Σ)∝exp{−21​(μ−μ0​)⊤Σ0−1​(μ−μ0​)}n=1∏N​exp{−21​(xn​−μ)⊤Σ−1(xn​−μ)}∝exp{−21​μ⊤JN​μ+μ⊤hN​},​​
(25)

where we have defined the posterior precision matrix JN=Σ0−1+NΣ−1\mbJ_N = \mbSigma_0^{-1} + N\mbSigma^{-1}JN​=Σ0−1​+NΣ−1 and posterior information vector hN=Σ0−1μ0+∑n=1NΣ−1xn\mbh_N = \mbSigma_0^{-1} \mbmu_0 + \sum_{n=1}^N \mbSigma^{-1} \mbx_nhN​=Σ0−1​μ0​+∑n=1N​Σ−1xn​. This has the form of a Gaussian in information parametrization (quadratic in μ\mbmuμ), so completing the square gives a Gaussian posterior,

p(μ∣X,η)=N(μ∣μN,ΣN),\begin{align} p(\mbmu \mid \mbX, \mbeta) &= \cN(\mbmu \mid \mbmu_N, \mbSigma_N), \end{align}p(μ∣X,η)​=N(μ∣μN​,ΣN​),​​
(26)

where

ΣN=JN−1=(Σ0−1+NΣ−1)−1,μN=ΣNhN=ΣN(Σ0−1μ0+NΣ−1xˉ),\begin{align} \mbSigma_N &= \mbJ_N^{-1} = (\mbSigma_0^{-1} + N \mbSigma^{-1})^{-1}, \\ \mbmu_N &= \mbSigma_N \mbh_N = \mbSigma_N \left(\mbSigma_0^{-1} \mbmu_0 + N \mbSigma^{-1} \bar{\mbx} \right), \end{align}ΣN​μN​​=JN−1​=(Σ0−1​+NΣ−1)−1,=ΣN​hN​=ΣN​(Σ0−1​μ0​+NΣ−1xˉ),​​
(27)

with xˉ=1N∑n=1Nxn\bar{\mbx} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^N \mbx_nxˉ=N1​∑n=1N​xn​ denoting the sample mean. The posterior precision is the sum of the prior precision and the data precision — information about the mean accumulates additively. The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the sample mean.

In the uninformative limit Σ0−1→0\mbSigma_0^{-1} \to \mbzeroΣ0−1​→0 (infinite prior uncertainty), the posterior mean converges to the sample mean xˉ\bar{\mbx}xˉ and the posterior covariance converges to 1NΣ\frac{1}{N}\mbSigmaN1​Σ — the distribution you would get from a Gaussian centered on the MLE.

The Wishart Distribution¶

Now suppose the mean μ\mbmuμ is known but the covariance (or equivalently, the precision) is unknown. To build intuition for the prior, recall how we handled the univariate case in Lecture 1: the χ2\chi^2χ2 distribution arose as the distribution of the sum of squared standard normal random variables. The Wishart distribution is the natural multivariate generalization of this construction.

Let z1,…,zν0\mbz_1, \ldots, \mbz_{\nu_0}z1​,…,zν0​​ be i.i.d. draws from a zero-mean multivariate normal,

zi∼iidN(0,Λ0),i=1,…,ν0.\begin{align} \mbz_i \iid{\sim} \cN(\mbzero, \mbLambda_0), \quad i = 1, \ldots, \nu_0. \end{align}zi​∼iidN(0,Λ0​),i=1,…,ν0​.​​
(28)

The sum of outer products Λ=∑i=1ν0zizi⊤\mbLambda = \sum_{i=1}^{\nu_0} \mbz_i \mbz_i^\topΛ=∑i=1ν0​​zi​zi⊤​ is a D×DD \times DD×D random positive semidefinite matrix. It follows a Wishart distribution with ν0\nu_0ν0​ degrees of freedom and scale Λ0\mbLambda_0Λ0​, written Λ∼W(ν0,Λ0)\mbLambda \sim \mathrm{W}(\nu_0, \mbLambda_0)Λ∼W(ν0​,Λ0​).

Note

There is an unfortunate asymmetry between this definition and the definition of the scaled χ2\chi^2χ2 distribution in Lecture 1. Whereas the scaled χ2\chi^2χ2 was the average of squared Gaussian random variables, the Wishart is the sum of outer products. This is to be consistent with standard textbook definitions, at the cost of a less symmetric relationship with the χ2\chi^2χ2 distribution.

Let SD\cS_DSD​ denote the set of D×DD \times DD×D symmetric positive definite matrices. For Λ∈SD\mbLambda \in \cS_DΛ∈SD​, the Wishart pdf is,

W(Λ∣ν0,Λ0)=12ν0D2∣Λ0∣ν02ΓD ⁣(ν02)∣Λ∣ν0−D−12exp⁡ ⁣(−12Tr(Λ0−1Λ)),\begin{align} \mathrm{W}(\mbLambda \mid \nu_0, \mbLambda_0) &= \frac{1}{2^{\frac{\nu_0 D}{2}}\left|\mbLambda_0\right|^{\frac{\nu_0}{2}}\Gamma_{D}\!\left({\frac{\nu_0}{2}}\right)} {\left| \mbLambda \right|}^{\frac{\nu_0-D-1}{2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} \Tr(\mbLambda_0^{-1}\mbLambda)\right), \end{align}W(Λ∣ν0​,Λ0​)​=22ν0​D​∣Λ0​∣2ν0​​ΓD​(2ν0​​)1​∣Λ∣2ν0​−D−1​exp(−21​Tr(Λ0−1​Λ)),​​
(29)

where ΓD\Gamma_DΓD​ is the multivariate gamma function, defined as

ΓD(a)=πD(D−1)/4∏d=1DΓ ⁣(a+1−d2).\begin{align} \Gamma_D(a) = \pi^{D(D-1)/4} \prod_{d=1}^D \Gamma\!\left(a + \frac{1-d}{2}\right). \end{align}ΓD​(a)=πD(D−1)/4d=1∏D​Γ(a+21−d​).​​
(30)

The parameters are: ν0>D−1\nu_0 > D - 1ν0​>D−1 degrees of freedom (which must exceed D−1D-1D−1 for the distribution to be proper) and Λ0∈SD\mbLambda_0 \in \cS_DΛ0​∈SD​, the scale matrix. The mean and mode are,

E[Λ]=ν0Λ0,andmode[Λ]=(ν0−D−1)Λ0for ν0≥D+1.\begin{align} \E[\mbLambda] &= \nu_0 \mbLambda_0, \quad \text{and} \quad \mathrm{mode}[\mbLambda] = (\nu_0 - D - 1)\mbLambda_0 \quad \text{for } \nu_0 \geq D+1. \end{align}E[Λ]​=ν0​Λ0​,andmode[Λ]=(ν0​−D−1)Λ0​for ν0​≥D+1.​​
(31)

In the univariate case D=1D=1D=1: W(λ∣ν0,ν0−1λ0)=χ2(ν0,λ0)\mathrm{W}(\lambda \mid \nu_0, \nu_0^{-1} \lambda_0) = \chi^2(\nu_0, \lambda_0)W(λ∣ν0​,ν0−1​λ0​)=χ2(ν0​,λ0​), which confirms the connection to the scaled chi-squared distribution.

The Wishart distribution plays a key role in two different settings. In frequentist statistics, it is the sampling distribution of the precision matrix estimated from multivariate normal data. In Bayesian statistics, it is the conjugate prior for the precision matrix of a multivariate normal, as we will see next.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

ν0 = torch.tensor(4.)
Λ0 = torch.eye(2) / ν0
Λs = Wishart(ν0, covariance_matrix=Λ0).sample(sample_shape=(5, 5))

fig, axs = plt.subplots(5, 5, figsize=(8, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for i in range(5):
    for j in range(5):
        plot_cov(torch.inverse(Λs[i, j]), ax=axs[i, j])
        plot_cov(torch.eye(2), ax=axs[i, j], color='k')
        axs[i, j].set_xlim(-4, 4)
        axs[i, j].set_ylim(-4, 4)
        axs[i, j].set_aspect(1)
for i in range(5):
    axs[i, 0].set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
    axs[-1, i].set_xlabel(r"$x_1$")
plt.tight_layout()
/opt/hostedtoolcache/Python/3.13.12/x64/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/distributions/distribution.py:173: UserWarning: Singular sample detected.
  return self.rsample(sample_shape)

Bayesian Estimation: Unknown Precision¶

With the Wishart distribution in hand, we can perform Bayesian inference for an unknown precision matrix:

Λ∼W(ν0,Λ0),xn∼iidN(μ,Λ−1).\begin{align} \mbLambda &\sim \mathrm{W}(\nu_0, \mbLambda_0), \\ \mbx_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbmu, \mbLambda^{-1}). \end{align}Λxn​​∼W(ν0​,Λ0​),∼iidN(μ,Λ−1).​​
(32)

To compute the posterior p(Λ∣X,η)p(\mbLambda \mid \mbX, \mbeta)p(Λ∣X,η) with η=(μ,ν0,Λ0)\mbeta = (\mbmu, \nu_0, \mbLambda_0)η=(μ,ν0​,Λ0​), we expand the Wishart prior and the Gaussian likelihood, collecting terms in Λ\mbLambdaΛ,

p(Λ∣X,η)∝W(Λ∣ν0,Λ0)∏n=1NN(xn∣μ,Λ−1)∝∣Λ∣ν0−D−12e−12Tr(Λ0−1Λ)⋅∏n=1N∣Λ∣12e−12(xn−μ)⊤Λ(xn−μ)∝∣Λ∣ν0+N−D−12exp⁡ ⁣(−12Tr ⁣([Λ0−1+∑n=1N(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤]Λ)).\begin{align} p(\mbLambda \mid \mbX, \mbeta) &\propto \mathrm{W}(\mbLambda \mid \nu_0, \mbLambda_0) \prod_{n=1}^N \cN(\mbx_n \mid \mbmu, \mbLambda^{-1}) \\ &\propto |\mbLambda|^{\frac{\nu_0 - D - 1}{2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2} \Tr(\mbLambda_0^{-1} \mbLambda)} \cdot \prod_{n=1}^N |\mbLambda|^{\frac{1}{2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2} (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbLambda (\mbx_n - \mbmu)} \\ &\propto |\mbLambda|^{\frac{\nu_0 + N - D - 1}{2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} \Tr\!\left(\left[\mbLambda_0^{-1} + \sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n -\mbmu) (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \right] \mbLambda \right)\right). \end{align}p(Λ∣X,η)​∝W(Λ∣ν0​,Λ0​)n=1∏N​N(xn​∣μ,Λ−1)∝∣Λ∣2ν0​−D−1​e−21​Tr(Λ0−1​Λ)⋅n=1∏N​∣Λ∣21​e−21​(xn​−μ)⊤Λ(xn​−μ)∝∣Λ∣2ν0​+N−D−1​exp(−21​Tr([Λ0−1​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤]Λ)).​​
(33)

In the last step we used the identity (xn−μ)⊤Λ(xn−μ)=Tr(Λ(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤)(\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \mbLambda (\mbx_n - \mbmu) = \Tr(\mbLambda (\mbx_n - \mbmu)(\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top)(xn​−μ)⊤Λ(xn​−μ)=Tr(Λ(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤) to consolidate the exponential. The result has exactly the form of a Wishart density, so the posterior is,

p(Λ∣X,η)=W(Λ∣νN,ΛN),\begin{align} p(\mbLambda \mid \mbX, \mbeta) &= \mathrm{W}(\mbLambda \mid \nu_N, \mbLambda_N), \end{align}p(Λ∣X,η)​=W(Λ∣νN​,ΛN​),​​
(34)

where

νN=ν0+N,ΛN=[Λ0−1+∑n=1N(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤]−1.\begin{align} \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \mbLambda_N &= \left[\mbLambda_0^{-1} + \sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n -\mbmu) (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top \right]^{-1}. \end{align}νN​ΛN​​=ν0​+N,=[Λ0−1​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤]−1.​​
(35)

The degrees of freedom simply accumulate: each new observation adds 1 to νN\nu_NνN​, increasing our certainty about Λ\mbLambdaΛ. The posterior scale ΛN\mbLambda_NΛN​ is the inverse of the sum of the prior inverse scale and the data scatter matrix ∑n(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤\sum_n (\mbx_n - \mbmu)(\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top∑n​(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤. In the large data limit, the posterior mean νNΛN\nu_N \mbLambda_NνN​ΛN​ converges to (1N∑n(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤)−1\bigl(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n} (\mbx_n - \mbmu)(\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top\bigr)^{-1}(N1​∑n​(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤)−1, the inverse of the sample covariance — as we would expect.

The Inverse Wishart Distribution¶

When it is more natural to parameterize in terms of the covariance Σ\mbSigmaΣ rather than the precision Λ\mbLambdaΛ, we use the inverse Wishart distribution. It is defined simply by a change of variables: if Λ∼W(ν0,Λ0)\mbLambda \sim \mathrm{W}(\nu_0, \mbLambda_0)Λ∼W(ν0​,Λ0​), then Σ=Λ−1\mbSigma = \mbLambda^{-1}Σ=Λ−1 follows an inverse Wishart distribution,

Λ∼W(ν0,Λ0)  ⟺  Σ=Λ−1∼IW(ν0,Σ0),\begin{align} \mbLambda \sim \mathrm{W}(\nu_0, \mbLambda_0) \iff \mbSigma = \mbLambda^{-1} \sim \mathrm{IW}(\nu_0, \mbSigma_0), \end{align}Λ∼W(ν0​,Λ0​)⟺Σ=Λ−1∼IW(ν0​,Σ0​),​​
(36)

where Σ0=Λ0−1\mbSigma_0 = \mbLambda_0^{-1}Σ0​=Λ0−1​. Its pdf is,

IW(Σ∣ν0,Σ0)=∣Σ0∣ν022ν0D2ΓD ⁣(ν02)∣Σ∣−ν0+D+12exp⁡ ⁣(−12Tr(Σ0Σ−1)).\begin{align} \mathrm{IW}(\mbSigma \mid \nu_0, \mbSigma_0) &= \frac{\left|\mbSigma_0\right|^{\frac{\nu_0}{2}}}{2^{\frac{\nu_0 D}{2}}\Gamma_{D}\!\left({\frac{\nu_0}{2}}\right)} {\left| \mbSigma \right|}^{-\frac{\nu_0+D+1}{2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} \Tr(\mbSigma_0 \mbSigma^{-1})\right). \end{align}IW(Σ∣ν0​,Σ0​)​=22ν0​D​ΓD​(2ν0​​)∣Σ0​∣2ν0​​​∣Σ∣−2ν0​+D+1​exp(−21​Tr(Σ0​Σ−1)).​​
(37)

The parameters are the same degrees of freedom ν0\nu_0ν0​ and a scale matrix Σ0∈SD\mbSigma_0 \in \cS_DΣ0​∈SD​. Its mean and mode are,

E[Σ]=Σ0ν0−D−1for ν0>D+1,andmode[Σ]=Σ0ν0+D+1.\begin{align} \E[\mbSigma] &= \frac{\mbSigma_0}{\nu_0 - D - 1} \quad \text{for } \nu_0 > D+1, \quad \text{and} \quad \mathrm{mode}[\mbSigma] = \frac{\mbSigma_0}{\nu_0 + D + 1}. \end{align}E[Σ]​=ν0​−D−1Σ0​​for ν0​>D+1,andmode[Σ]=ν0​+D+1Σ0​​.​​
(38)

The scale matrix Σ0\mbSigma_0Σ0​ can be interpreted as a prior sum of squared deviations, and ν0\nu_0ν0​ controls how tightly concentrated the prior is around Σ0ν0−D−1\frac{\mbSigma_0}{\nu_0 - D - 1}ν0​−D−1Σ0​​. In the univariate case D=1D=1D=1: IW(σ2∣ν0,ν0σ02)=χ−2(ν0,σ02)\mathrm{IW}(\sigma^2 \mid \nu_0, \nu_0 \sigma_0^2) = \chi^{-2}(\nu_0, \sigma_0^2)IW(σ2∣ν0​,ν0​σ02​)=χ−2(ν0​,σ02​), recovering the scaled inverse chi-squared from Lecture 1.

Exercise

Consider the model with unknown covariance,

Σ∼IW(ν0,Σ0),xn∼iidN(μ,Σ).\begin{align} \mbSigma &\sim \mathrm{IW}(\nu_0, \mbSigma_0), \\ \mbx_n &\iid{\sim} \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma). \end{align}Σxn​​∼IW(ν0​,Σ0​),∼iidN(μ,Σ).​​
(39)

Show that the posterior is also inverse Wishart,

p(Σ∣X,η)=IW(νN,ΣN),\begin{align} p(\mbSigma \mid \mbX, \mbeta) &= \mathrm{IW}(\nu_N, \mbSigma_N), \end{align}p(Σ∣X,η)​=IW(νN​,ΣN​),​​
(40)

where

νN=ν0+N,ΣN=Σ0+∑n=1N(xn−μ)(xn−μ)⊤.\begin{align} \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \mbSigma_N &= \mbSigma_0 + \sum_{n=1}^N (\mbx_n - \mbmu) (\mbx_n - \mbmu)^\top. \end{align}νN​ΣN​​=ν0​+N,=Σ0​+n=1∑N​(xn​−μ)(xn​−μ)⊤.​​
(41)

Interpret the update: ΣN\mbSigma_NΣN​ is the prior scale matrix plus the empirical scatter matrix.

Source
torch.manual_seed(305)

N  = 10
X  = MultivariateNormal(torch.zeros(2), torch.eye(2)).sample((N,))

ν0 = torch.tensor(1.0)
Λ0 = torch.eye(2)
νN = ν0 + N
ΛN = torch.inverse(torch.inverse(Λ0) + X.T @ X)

Λ_samp = Wishart(νN, ΛN).sample((10,))
Σ_samp = torch.inverse(Λ_samp)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.set_aspect(1)
for i, Σ in enumerate(Σ_samp):
    plot_cov(Σ, ax=ax, color='r', alpha=0.5,
             label=r"$\Sigma$ samples" if i == 0 else None)
ax.plot(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], 'ko', markersize=6, label="data")
ax.set_xlim(-4, 4)
ax.set_ylim(-4, 4)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()

Normal-Inverse-Wishart Prior¶

In the most general setting, both μ\mbmuμ and Σ\mbSigmaΣ are unknown. Following the same strategy as Lecture 1 for the univariate case, we construct a joint conjugate prior over (μ,Σ)(\mbmu, \mbSigma)(μ,Σ). The key idea is to express the joint prior as a product p(μ,Σ)=p(μ∣Σ) p(Σ)p(\mbmu, \mbSigma) = p(\mbmu \mid \mbSigma) \, p(\mbSigma)p(μ,Σ)=p(μ∣Σ)p(Σ), choosing each factor to be conjugate in a compatible way.

Specifically, we set the prior on the covariance to be inverse Wishart and — crucially — make the prior on the mean conditional on the covariance: μ∣Σ∼N(μ0,Σ/κ0)\mbmu \mid \mbSigma \sim \cN(\mbmu_0, \mbSigma / \kappa_0)μ∣Σ∼N(μ0​,Σ/κ0​). This coupling between the mean and covariance priors, where the uncertainty in μ\mbmuμ scales with Σ/κ0\mbSigma / \kappa_0Σ/κ0​, is what makes the joint prior conjugate. The result is the normal-inverse-Wishart (NIW) distribution,

NIW(μ,Σ∣μ0,κ0,ν0,Σ0)=N(μ∣μ0,Σ/κ0) IW(Σ∣ν0,Σ0).\begin{align} \mathrm{NIW}(\mbmu, \mbSigma \mid \mbmu_0, \kappa_0, \nu_0, \mbSigma_0) &= \cN(\mbmu \mid \mbmu_0, \mbSigma / \kappa_0) \, \mathrm{IW}(\mbSigma \mid \nu_0, \mbSigma_0). \end{align}NIW(μ,Σ∣μ0​,κ0​,ν0​,Σ0​)​=N(μ∣μ0​,Σ/κ0​)IW(Σ∣ν0​,Σ0​).​​
(42)

The four hyperparameters have natural interpretations: μ0\mbmu_0μ0​ is the prior mean, κ0\kappa_0κ0​ is the mean confidence (how many pseudo-observations worth of information we have about the mean), ν0\nu_0ν0​ is the degrees of freedom for the covariance, and Σ0\mbSigma_0Σ0​ is the prior scale for the covariance.

Exercise

Consider the model (μ,Σ)∼NIW(μ0,κ0,ν0,Σ0)(\mbmu, \mbSigma) \sim \mathrm{NIW}(\mbmu_0, \kappa_0, \nu_0, \mbSigma_0)(μ,Σ)∼NIW(μ0​,κ0​,ν0​,Σ0​) and xn∼iidN(μ,Σ)\mbx_n \iid{\sim} \cN(\mbmu, \mbSigma)xn​∼iidN(μ,Σ). Show that the posterior is,

p(μ,Σ∣X)=NIW(μN,κN,νN,ΣN),\begin{align} p(\mbmu, \mbSigma \mid \mbX) &= \mathrm{NIW}(\mbmu_N, \kappa_N, \nu_N, \mbSigma_N), \end{align}p(μ,Σ∣X)​=NIW(μN​,κN​,νN​,ΣN​),​​
(43)

where

κN=κ0+N,νN=ν0+N,μN=κ0μ0+∑n=1NxnκN=κ0κNμ0+NκNxˉ,ΣN=Σ0+κ0μ0μ0⊤+∑n=1Nxnxn⊤−κNμNμN⊤.\begin{align} \kappa_N &= \kappa_0 + N, \\ \nu_N &= \nu_0 + N, \\ \mbmu_N &= \frac{\kappa_0 \mbmu_0 + \sum_{n=1}^N \mbx_n}{\kappa_N} = \frac{\kappa_0}{\kappa_N} \mbmu_0 + \frac{N}{\kappa_N} \bar{\mbx}, \\ \mbSigma_N &= \mbSigma_0 + \kappa_0 \mbmu_0\mbmu_0^\top + \sum_{n=1}^N \mbx_n \mbx_n^\top - \kappa_N \mbmu_N \mbmu_N^\top. \end{align}κN​νN​μN​ΣN​​=κ0​+N,=ν0​+N,=κN​κ0​μ0​+∑n=1N​xn​​=κN​κ0​​μ0​+κN​N​xˉ,=Σ0​+κ0​μ0​μ0⊤​+n=1∑N​xn​xn⊤​−κN​μN​μN⊤​.​​
(44)

Notice that μN\mbmu_NμN​ is a weighted average of the prior mean μ0\mbmu_0μ0​ and the sample mean xˉ\bar{\mbx}xˉ, with weights κ0/κN\kappa_0 / \kappa_Nκ0​/κN​ and N/κNN / \kappa_NN/κN​ respectively.

Posterior Marginals¶

Just as in the univariate case (where the marginal of the mean under a normal-inverse-chi-squared posterior was a Student’s t distribution), the posterior marginal of μ\mbmuμ under the NIW is a multivariate Student’s t.

Integrating out Σ\mbSigmaΣ,

p(μ∣X)=∫p(μ,Σ∣X) ⁣dΣ=∫N(μ∣μN,Σ/κN) IW(Σ∣νN,ΣN) ⁣dΣ=St ⁣(νN−D+1,  μN,  ΣNκN(νN−D+1)).\begin{align} p(\mbmu \mid \mbX) &= \int p(\mbmu, \mbSigma \mid \mbX) \dif \mbSigma \\ &= \int \cN(\mbmu \mid \mbmu_N, \mbSigma / \kappa_N) \, \mathrm{IW}(\mbSigma \mid \nu_N, \mbSigma_N) \dif \mbSigma \\ &= \mathrm{St}\!\left(\nu_N - D + 1,\; \mbmu_N,\; \frac{\mbSigma_N}{\kappa_N (\nu_N - D + 1)} \right). \end{align}p(μ∣X)​=∫p(μ,Σ∣X)dΣ=∫N(μ∣μN​,Σ/κN​)IW(Σ∣νN​,ΣN​)dΣ=St(νN​−D+1,μN​,κN​(νN​−D+1)ΣN​​).​​
(45)

The Student’s t distribution arises because we are averaging a Gaussian over a random covariance drawn from an inverse Wishart — the extra uncertainty about Σ\mbSigmaΣ inflates the tails of the marginal compared to a pure Gaussian. The multivariate Student’s t distribution with ν\nuν degrees of freedom, location μ\mbmuμ, and scale matrix Σ\mbSigmaΣ has density,

St(x∣ν,μ,Σ)=Γ ⁣(ν+D2)Γ ⁣(ν2)(νπ)−D2∣Σ∣−12[1+Δ2ν]−ν+D2,\begin{align} \mathrm{St}(\mbx \mid \nu, \mbmu, \mbSigma) &= \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu + D}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)} (\nu \pi)^{-\frac{D}{2}} |\mbSigma|^{-\frac{1}{2}} \left[1 + \frac{\Delta^2}{\nu} \right]^{-\frac{\nu + D}{2}}, \end{align}St(x∣ν,μ,Σ)​=Γ(2ν​)Γ(2ν+D​)​(νπ)−2D​∣Σ∣−21​[1+νΔ2​]−2ν+D​,​​
(46)

where Δ2=(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ)\Delta^2 = (\mbx - \mbmu)^\top \mbSigma^{-1} (\mbx - \mbmu)Δ2=(x−μ)⊤Σ−1(x−μ) is the squared Mahalanobis distance. When ν\nuν is large, the term [1+Δ2/ν]−(ν+D)/2[1 + \Delta^2/\nu]^{-(\nu+D)/2}[1+Δ2/ν]−(ν+D)/2 converges to e−Δ2/2e^{-\Delta^2/2}e−Δ2/2 and the Student’s t approaches a Gaussian. When ν\nuν is small, the distribution has heavier tails than a Gaussian. The mean of the multivariate Student’s t is μ\mbmuμ and the covariance is νν−2Σ\frac{\nu}{\nu - 2} \mbSigmaν−2ν​Σ (for ν>2\nu > 2ν>2) — the extra factor of ν/(ν−2)>1\nu / (\nu - 2) > 1ν/(ν−2)>1 reflects the heavier tails.

In our posterior, the effective degrees of freedom νN−D+1=ν0+N−D+1\nu_N - D + 1 = \nu_0 + N - D + 1νN​−D+1=ν0​+N−D+1 grow with the number of observations, so the posterior predictive distribution converges to a Gaussian as N→∞N \to \inftyN→∞, as we would expect when we become certain about Σ\mbSigmaΣ.

Reading

Bishop, 2006, Ch 2.3. See also: Murphy, 2023, Ch 2.3 and 3.2.4.

Conclusion¶

The multivariate normal distribution is the cornerstone of high-dimensional probabilistic modeling. Its key analytical properties — the ability to marginalize and condition in closed form, the Schur complement formula for conditional means and variances, and the Woodbury identity for efficient precision-form updates — appear repeatedly throughout the course. The conjugate prior framework generalizes naturally from the scalar case: the Wishart and inverse-Wishart distributions are the natural conjugates for the precision and covariance respectively, and the normal-inverse-Wishart prior gives a joint conjugate for unknown mean and covariance. The posterior marginal of the mean is a multivariate Student’s t, reflecting additional uncertainty from integrating out the unknown covariance.

Next Steps

  • Hierarchical Gaussian Models — applies the multivariate Gaussian identities to derive exact posteriors in a multi-level model

  • Probabilistic PCA — uses the MVN as both the latent prior and the observation model in a linear generative model

  • Linear Dynamical Systems — the Kalman filter is entirely built from the conditional and marginal identities derived here

  • Gaussian Processes — a GP is an infinite-dimensional generalization of the MVN; all GP computations reduce to linear algebra with the covariance matrix

Recommended Reading

Bishop, 2006, Ch 2.3. See also: Murphy, 2023, Ch 2.3 and 3.2.4.

References¶
  1. Bishop, C. M. (2006). Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer.
  2. Murphy, K. P. (2023). Probabilistic Machine Learning: Advanced Topics. MIT Press. https://probml.github.io/pml-book/book2.html

Bayesian Analysis of the Normal Distribution

Probabilistic Graphical Models
